title: Choking
summary: Choking occurs when a foreign object lodges in the throat, blocking the airway. It can be fatal within minutes without intervention.
steps: Ask the person 'Are you choking?' — If they can cough, encourage them to keep coughing.
If they cannot cough, speak, or breathe — call for emergency help immediately.
Bend the person forward and give up to 5 firm back blows between the shoulder blades with the heel of your hand.
Check their mouth after each blow — remove any visible obstruction.
If back blows don't work, give up to 5 abdominal thrusts (Heimlich maneuver).
Place your fist (thumb side) just above the navel, below the ribs. Grasp with the other hand and thrust sharply upward.
Alternate 5 back blows and 5 abdominal thrusts until the object is dislodged or help arrives.
If the person becomes unconscious, begin CPR immediately.
what_to_do: Encourage coughing if the person can still breathe
Give back blows between shoulder blades
Call emergency services if the obstruction is severe
Start CPR if the person loses consciousness
what_not_to_do: Do NOT perform blind finger sweeps in the mouth
Do NOT give liquid to a choking person
Do NOT leave the person alone
Do NOT perform abdominal thrusts on infants — use back blows only
Do NOT apply abdominal thrusts on pregnant women — use chest thrusts
when_to_call_ambulance: The obstruction does not clear with back blows and abdominal thrusts
The person becomes unconscious or stops breathing
The choking victim is an infant or child
The person is pregnant or obese (use chest thrusts instead)
Person has a history of respiratory illness or heart problems
